description: This chart presents the regression coefficients (r_i, in GPa per mass%) quantifying the influence of individual elements on the elastic modulus of martensitic and ferritic steels. The horizontal axis lists elements in order of decreasing statistical significance (r_i/σ_i): Fe, Cr, Mo, Si, Ni, Mn, Cu, and C. Black squares with vertical error bars denote the regression coefficients from a study of 53 steel batches, while open circles mark literature data points. Si shows the strongest positive effect (~4.6 GPa), followed by Cr (~2.7 GPa) and Mo (~2.6 GPa). Fe provides a baseline near 2.1 GPa. Ni and C exhibit near-zero coefficients, and Mn and Cu display large uncertainties, rendering them statistically insignificant. The caption states that Cr, Mo, and Si increase the elastic modulus, whereas C and Ni decrease it, with the model achieving R² = 0.96 and a standard error of 1.2 GPa. One literature value for C deviates sharply to approximately -25 GPa, illustrating data scatter. The figure supports the conclusion that solid-solution strengthening by specific elements reliably predicts the elastic modulus when phase and microstructure effects are neglected.

Figure 2 is a regression-analysis plot illustrating the compositional influence on the elastic modulus of ferritic steels, where ferrite elemental concentrations and cementite contents were calculated with Thermo-Calc. The horizontal axis lists Fe, cementite, Cr, Mo, Si, Mn, and Cu in order of decreasing statistical significance (r_i/σ_i), and the vertical axis shows the regression coefficient r_i in GPa. Horizontal bars with error bars denote the fitted coefficients and their standard errors, while open circles represent literature data points. Si and Mn exhibit the strongest positive effects (approximately 5.5 and 5.4 GPa per mass%, respectively), followed by Mo (~3.9 GPa) and Cr (~2.2 GPa); Fe and cementite both lie near ~2.1 GPa. The caption reports that cementite, despite its statistical significance, has virtually no effect on the elastic modulus, and that the weak positive effect of Cu is insignificant owing to its large standard error. The accompanying text states the regression model achieves R² = 0.85 with a standard error of 1.1 GPa.

Figure 3 displays a bar-and-error-bar plot of regression coefficients r_i (in GPa) quantifying the effect of individual components on the elastic modulus of austenitic steels, ordered by decreasing statistical significance (r_i/σ_i). Visual data points with error bars (standard deviations) are shown for Fe (~2.0 GPa), Cr (~2.4 GPa), Ni (~1.9 GPa), NbC (~6.5 GPa), M23C6 (Cr23C6, ~1.9 GPa), TiC (~7.3 GPa), interstitial N (~15.1 GPa with the largest uncertainty), and Mo (~0.5 GPa). While all plotted coefficients appear positive, caption states that Ni and Mo decrease the elastic modulus, whereas N, NbC, TiC, and Cr increase it; M23C6 is reported as statistically significant yet effectively negligible. The analysis covers 24 steel batches with austenite compositions and carbide phase contents calculated using Thermo-Calc. The regression model yields R² = 0.83, a standard error of 2.9 GPa, a maximum deviation of 3.1 GPa, and an average deviation of 1.1 GPa. The figure demonstrates that carbide phases and nitrogen exert the strongest positive influences, although N suffers from low significance due to limited alloy batches.
